questions.text: An economy’s gross domestic product is …..
options: the excess of spending over income.
the excess of income over spending.
total spending as a percent of total income.

questions.text: Kamola bakes a cake and sells it to Normurod for $10. Samandar pays Dilnoza $30 to tutor him. In this economy, GDP is …..
If the price of a hot dog is $2 and the price of a hamburger is $4, then 30 hot dogs contribute as much to GDP as _____ hamburgers.

Aziz the sheep farmer sells wool to Dilnoza the knitter for $20. Dilnoza makes two sweaters, each of which has a market price of $40. Charos buys one of them, while the other remains on the shelf of Dilnoza’s store to be sold later. What is GDP here?
Dilnoza, an Uzbek student, moves to Japan for the summer to teach English. Her salary is included
options: in both Uzbekistan’s GDP and Japan’s GDP
in neither Uzbekistan’s GDP nor Japan’s GDP

questions.text: Which of the following does NOT add to Uzbekistan’s GDP?
options: Artel manufactures and sells a fridge to Kazakhstan.
GM Uzbekistan builds a new auto factory in Syrdarya
The government pays a salary to a policeman
#The government sends a Social Security check to your grandmother

questions.text: An Uzbek buys a pair of shoes made in China. How do Uzbekistan’s national income accounts treat the transaction?
options: Net exports and GDP both rise.
Net exports and GDP both fall.
#Net exports fall, while GDP does not change.
Net exports do not change, while GDP rises.

questions.text: Which is the largest component of GDP?
GDP is an imperfect measure of well-being because it
options: includes physical goods produced but not intangible services.
excludes goods and services provided by the government.
#ignores the environmental degradation from economic activity.
is not correlated with other measures of the quality of life.

questions.text: Which of the following is included in the calculation of GDP?
options: the sale of a used car
#a new factory built by a company

questions.text: The four components of GDP in the expenditure approach are:
options: consumption, net export, wages, investment, government purchases

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a component of investment spending in GDP calculations?
The difference between nominal GDP and real GDP is:
options: Nominal GDP is adjusted for inflation, real GDP is not
#Real GDP is adjusted for inflation, nominal GDP is not
Nominal GDP measures only production, real GDP includes services

questions.text: If a country’s nominal GDP increases while its real GDP remains unchanged, what has likely happened?
options: More goods and services have been produced

questions.text: Which type of goods are excluded from GDP to avoid double counting?
If a country's GDP grows at 5% per year, approximately how long will it take for GDP to double?
If nominal GDP in 2023 is $10 trillion and the GDP deflator is 125, what is real GDP (base year = 100)?
A country experiences a 4% increase in its nominal GDP, but inflation rises by 2% in the same period. What is the approximate real GDP growth rate?
What does the phrase “the market value” mean in the GDP definition?
options: #Goods and services are measured using the prices buyers actually pay
Output is recorded only in terms of how many units are produced
Goods are counted based on production costs rather than sales prices
Prices are included only when they are set by government agencies

questions.text: In the GDP definition, what does the phrase “of all” indicate?
options: #GDP covers every legal final item that the economy produces and sells
GDP includes only the main categories of goods sold by large industries
GDP records the items governments decide to classify as major outputs
GDP counts goods that appear in markets while ignoring most services

questions.text: In the GDP definition, what does the term “final” mean?
options: Goods purchased for use in further production
Items kept by firms for possible later sale
#Goods acquired for direct consumption by users
Products traded while still in early market stages

questions.text: In the GDP definition, what does the term “produced” refer to?
options: Goods exchanged in markets without creating new output
Items held by firms and offered later for potential resale
#Goods newly created within the current production period
Products transferred between owners without additional work

questions.text: In the GDP definition, what does the phrase “within a country” mean?
options: Goods created by firms following local rules
Items made by workers living in the country
Production carried out under domestic control

questions.text: What does Gross national product (GNP) measure?
options: Income earned from production inside national borders
#Earnings received by residents from all global sources
Output created within the territory of the economy
Revenue generated by firms operating in the country

questions.text: What does Net national product (NNP) represent?
options: #Income after accounting for reductions in capital
Value of output before subtracting depreciation
Earnings measured without adjusting asset wear
Production totals recorded before capital losses

questions.text: What does Personal Income include?
options: Payments received by households from all sources
Income obtained after removing indirect taxes
Funds collected by households from market activity

questions.text: What is Disposable personal income?
options: #Income left after households pay personal taxes
Earnings before subtracting any required payments
Funds retained after deducting business expenses
Revenue received without considering tax charges

questions.text: What is the most commonly used measure of the cost of living?
The Consumer price index (CPI) measures:
options: The total value of goods and services produced within a country's economy
The overall amount of money people spend on products and services
#A statistical measure of how prices change over time in the consumer market
The fluctuation in costs of key raw materials used in production

questions.text: The formula for calculating the CPI is:
options: #(Price of basket in current year / Price of basket in base year) × 100
(Price of basket in current year - Price of basket in base year) / Price of basket in base year × 100
(Price of basket in current year - Price of basket in base year) × 100

questions.text: What is the inflation rate if the CPI was 120 last year and is 126 this year?
Which of the following is a problem with measuring the cost of living using CPI?
options: It does not include food prices
#It ignores changes in the quality of goods
It is based on GDP rather than consumer spending
It does not consider the effects of imports

questions.text: When consumers switch from expensive brands to cheaper alternatives, this is called:
If the price of gasoline rises and consumers start using public transport more, how does this affect CPI?
Which of the following is a key difference between the CPI and the GDP deflator?
options: The GDP deflator includes only imported goods
#The GDP deflator measures all goods produced domestically, while CPI includes imports
CPI measures production, while the GDP deflator measures consumption
The GDP deflator is based on fixed weights, while CPI changes over time

questions.text: If nominal interest rates are 8% and inflation is 3%, what is the real interest rate?
Suppose a pensioner receives a fixed income of 1200 thousand per year. If inflation is 6%, what will be his or her real income next year?
The CPI measures approximately the same economic phenomenon as
The largest component in the basket of goods and services used to compute the CPI is
If a tractor manufacturer raises the price, its price hike will increase
options: both the CPI and the GDP deflator.
neither the CPI nor the GDP deflator.
the CPI but not the GDP deflator.
#the GDP deflator but not the CPI.

questions.text: Indexation refers to:
options: A rule that keeps certain wages unchanged over time
#An automatic change in payments to offset inflation
A shift in prices mainly driven by supply conditions
A contract section that restricts future wage growth

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of indexation?
options: A worker gets a bonus for good work
A loan keeps the same interest rate for years
#A pension rises each year when prices rise
A store changes its prices every two years

questions.text: The nominal interest rate is:
options: The interest rate after subtracting inflation
#The interest rate before any inflation correction
The rate banks pay only on savings accounts
The rate that shows the real return over time

questions.text: The real interest rate is:
options: The rate reported by banks in advertisements
The rate charged only on long-term loans

questions.text: If inflation rises, the real interest rate:
options: Stays the same no matter what
#decreases if the nominal rate stays the same

questions.text: Which statement is true?
options: Nominal rate always equals real rate
Real rate is always higher than nominal

questions.text: If the CPI is 200 for the year 2010 and 300 today, then $600 in 2010 has the same purchasing power as ________ has today.

You deposit $2,000 in a savings account, and a year later, you have $2,100. Meanwhile, the CPI rises from 200 to 204. In this case, the nominal interest rate is _____ percent, and the real interest rate is _____ percent.
You deposit $1,500 in a savings account, and one year later you have $1,590. During the same year, the CPI increases from 250 to 260. What are the nominal interest rate and the real interest rate?
What does productivity measure in an economy?
options: The overall size of the workforce in an economy
#The level of production achieved relative to the amount of labor used
The total supply of money available within an economic system
The total area of land designated for agricultural activities

questions.text: Which of the following is a key factor that determines a nation’s standard of living?
options: The amount of money printed by the government

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a direct determinant of productivity?
Human capital refers to:
options: The structures, tools, and equipment involved in the production process
#The abilities, expertise, and training that individuals gain over time
The overall quantity of financial resources circulating in an economy
The total number of people residing within a nation

questions.text: The "catch-up effect" suggests that:
options: #Poorer countries tend to grow faster than richer countries under similar conditions
Richer countries always grow faster than poorer countries under similar conditions
Economic growth rates are the same across all nations under similar conditions
Human capital does not affect growth under similar conditions

questions.text: A nation with a high population growth rate but slow capital accumulation is likely to experience:
options: A rapid increase in per capita income

questions.text: If a country's capital stock increases by 10% and its output increases by 5%, what does this suggest?
options: The economy is experiencing increasing returns to capital
#The economy is experiencing diminishing returns to capital
The economy is in a steady state
The economy is over-investing in capital

questions.text: From 2010 to 2025, real GDP per person in Uzbekistan ________.
Higher productivity means:
options: Workers need more hours to produce the same output
#Each worker produces more output per unit of time
More workers are hired even if output stays the same

questions.text: Physical capital refers to:
options: Money that a firm keeps for expenses
#Tools and buildings used to make goods
Natural items found in the environment

questions.text: Which is an example of physical capital?
Which is an example of human capital?
options: A firm borrowing money from a bank

questions.text: Human capital increases when:
options: Firms reduce the number of employees
Wages stay the same for many years

questions.text: Which statement is true about natural resources and living standards?
options: Only countries with many natural resources can become rich
Countries without natural resources cannot trade internationally
#Natural resources help, but they are not required for high productivity
Economies with fewer resources must stop producing goods

questions.text: Technological knowledge means:
options: Hiring extra workers to finish tasks faster
Using more land to increase total output
Saving money to expand future production

questions.text: Which is an example of technological knowledge?
options: #Creating a quicker method to make a product
Buying new land for future production use
Adding more workers to do the same job
Renting extra space for storing materials

questions.text: Technological knowledge increases when:
options: #New ideas improve how goods are made
Firms replace training with extra paperwork
Workers repeat the same old procedures

questions.text: Why does technological knowledge raise productivity?
options: #It helps firms produce goods more efficiently
It requires using more energy for each output
It forces firms to spend less on equipment
It makes workers do more tasks manually

questions.text: Increases in the amount of human capital in the economy tend to ________ real incomes because they increase the ________ of labor.
Why must a society save more if it wants to increase its future capital stock?
options: #Because using resources for capital means fewer goods for today
Because higher capital always raises current consumption quickly
Because saving allows workers to buy more goods each day
Because firms cannot produce capital without government spending

questions.text: What is one reason saving and investment help long-run growth?
options: #They increase the economy’s ability to produce in the future
They remove the need for workers to use skills or training
They allow households to avoid trade-offs between choices
They reduce the role of financial markets in the economy

questions.text: Diminishing returns means that:
options: #Each extra unit of an input adds less output than before
Each extra unit of an input always doubles total output
Extra input raises output at a faster and faster rate
Output stays the same no matter how much input is added

questions.text: Which situation shows diminishing returns?
options: #Adding more workers increases output, but by smaller amounts
Adding more machines raises output by the same amount each time
Hiring more labor always increases output at the same speed
Adding workers does nothing because the firm never changes output

questions.text: What is foreign direct investment?
options: #When a foreign firm builds a business in another country
When a country sells goods to foreign buyers through trade
When workers move to another country for temporary jobs
When banks exchange money between different countries

questions.text: What is foreign portfolio investment?
options: #When foreigners buy stocks and bonds in another country
When a company builds new offices in another country of the world
When governments borrow from each other for short periods
When tourists spend money while visiting other countries

questions.text: What is the problem of brain drain for poor countries?
options: #Educated workers leave, reducing the country’s human capital
Workers travel abroad only to buy goods and return home
Students study overseas but always come back immediately
Families stop sending children to school because education is free

questions.text: Why can health be considered a form of human capital?
options: #Good health helps people work more effectively and raise output
Good health removes the need for education and training entirely
Good health ensures that all workers earn exactly the same wage
Good health allows firms to avoid buying machines and tools

questions.text: According to Fogel’s findings, why did productivity rise in Great Britain?
options: #Better nutrition improved workers’ ability to perform physical labor
Workers spent more time on leisure activities and less on work tasks
People became shorter as food supplies became more limited
Firms reduced investment in physical capital, tools and new machinery

questions.text: What does an increase in average height usually signal for a population?
options: #Better nutrition and improved living conditions over time
A major change in the genetic structure of the population
A shift away from manual work toward office employment
An increase in pollution and other environmental problems

questions.text: What is one reason poor countries remain trapped in a cycle of poverty?
options: #Poor health lowers productivity, and low income limits access to better care
Poor health makes workers avoid travel, even when jobs are available abroad
Poor health leads firms to change tasks, even when work levels stay the same
Poor health causes wages to shift, even when output stays unchanged

questions.text: Why are property rights important for economic growth?
options: #They give firms confidence that their resources will be protected
They require firms to hire more workers for every production stage
They ensure that prices stay the same even when markets change
They force producers to sell goods only to domestic consumers

questions.text: What is one problem with inward-oriented policies?
options: #They limit trade and reduce the gains from global exchange
They help firms compete more easily with foreign producers
They allow new industries to grow without any government support
They increase productivity by raising foreign investment flows

questions.text: Why do economists favor outward-oriented policies?
options: #They integrate countries into world markets and support faster growth
They help governments control prices more easily in local markets within the country
They reduce the need for technology and make production simpler
They allow firms to avoid competition and maintain protected markets

questions.text: According to Malthus, why would population growth lead to permanent poverty?
options: #Because population would grow faster than food production
Because technological progress would stop as more people were born into the lower social classes
Because governments and charities would expand support programs that encouraged unlimited family size
Because agricultural land would decline sharply and farmers would be unable to increase output despite rising demand

questions.text: Why did Malthus’s prediction ultimately turn out to be wrong?
options: #Because technological progress greatly increased food production
Because global population growth unexpectedly slowed and the number of farmers expanded faster than expected
Because modern economies shifted away from agriculture, eliminating the need for rising food output
Because international trade solved all food shortages and countries no longer relied on domestic farming

questions.text: What is the financial system?
options: A network of banks controlled by the government
#A group of institutions that help match savings with investments
A system used only by businesses to track their income
A method of printing money in an economy

questions.text: What is investment in the context of macroeconomics?
options: Spending money on financial assets like shares and government securities
Using income to acquire products and services for everyday consumption
#The purchase of new capital goods, such as equipment and buildings
Placing financial resources into a secure account for future use

questions.text: What is the role of banks in the financial system?
options: Serving primarily as a place where individuals can keep their money securely
#Playing a key role in connecting those who save money with those in need of funds for economic activities
Being responsible for the creation and distribution of new monetary units in an economy
Overseeing and implementing policies related to government revenue collection

questions.text: What is the main function of the bond market?
options: Enabling authorities to generate revenue by adjusting tax rates on individuals and businesses
#Providing businesses and governments with a way to borrow money
Acting as a secure facility for safeguarding precious metals on behalf of investors
Establishing the official exchange rate and overall worth of the national currency

questions.text: What is the difference between stocks and bonds?
options: #Holding shares means having a stake in a business, whereas purchasing debt securities means lending funds to corporations or public institutions
Fixed-income securities generally carry higher financial uncertainty compared to equity investments
Corporate ownership instruments guarantee regular interest earnings, while debt obligations distribute company profits to holders
Debt instruments signify partial control of an entity, whereas equity securities function as financial

questions.text: The equation for national saving in a closed economy is:
If a government has a budget surplus, this means:
options: Government spending is greater than tax revenue
Government spending is equal to tax revenue
#Tax revenue is greater than government spending

questions.text: Suppose GDP equals $10 trillion, consumption equals $6.5 trillion, the government spends $2 trillion and has a budget deficit of $300 billion. Find private saving.
What is the crowding-out effect?
options: When private investment increases due to lower interest rates
#When government borrowing reduces the funds available for private investment
When stock prices increase due to high demand in the economy
When savers invest more money in government bonds than in private stocks

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a financial intermediary?
If an economy has output (Y) of $10 bln, consumption (of $6 bln, and government spending (G) of $2 bln, what is national saving?
If the real interest rate increases, what is the likely effect on saving and investment?
Suppose a government increases borrowing by issuing bonds. What is the likely effect on interest rates?
Why do long-term bonds generally pay higher interest rates than short-term bonds?
options: #Because long-term bonds carry greater price and liquidity risk
Because governments require higher payments on long-term bonds

questions.text: When will a bond typically offer a higher interest rate?
options: When the issuer has an AAA credit rating
When the issuer has strong financial stability
#When the probability of default is high
When the bond is issued by the federal government

questions.text: Why do municipal bonds usually pay lower interest rates than corporate bonds?
options: Because they have higher credit risk
#Because their interest income is tax free

questions.text: What distinguishes equity finance from debt finance?
options: #Equity finance involves selling ownership shares, while debt finance involves issuing bonds.
Equity finance requires fixed interest payments, while debt finance does not.
Equity finance gives lenders repayment priority in bankruptcy.
Debt finance grants investors voting rights in the company.

questions.text: Why do stocks generally involve greater risk than bonds?
options: Stockholders receive fixed payments, while bondholders receive profits.
Bondholders share directly in company profits.
#Stockholders are paid after bondholders in financial distress.
Bondholders have ownership rights in the company.

questions.text: Aziza wants to publish a local newspaper but doesn’t have the financial resources to start the business. She borrows $60,000 from her friend Kamron, to whom she promises an interest rate of 7 percent, and gets another $40,000 from her friend Javohir, to whom she promises 10 percent of her profits. What best describes this situation?
options: Kamron is a stockholder, and Aziza is a bondholder.
Kamron is a stockholder, and Javohir is a bondholder.
Javohir is a stockholder, and Aziza is a bondholder.
#Javohir is a stockholder, and Kamron is a bondholder.

questions.text: Which best defines private saving?
What is public saving?
A budget surplus occurs when:
A budget deficit occurs when:
If government spending rises while taxes stay the same, what is most likely to happen?
If households pay taxes and then spend less on consumption, what increases?
When public saving is positive, it means:
National saving consists of:
If the government collects more in tax revenue than it spends, and households consume more than they get in after-tax income, then
options: private saving and public saving are both positive.
private saving and public saving are both negative.
private saving is positive, but public saving is negative.
#private saving is negative, but public saving is positive.

questions.text: A closed economy has income of $1,000, govern ment spending of $200, taxes of $150, and invest ment of $250. What is private saving?
What is the main effect of saving incentives?
options: #They raise the after-tax return to saving.

questions.text: What is the purpose of an investment tax credit?
options: To reduce taxes on household income.
#To lower the cost of business investment.
To raise taxes on capital goods.

questions.text: When does a government have a balanced budget?
If a government runs a balanced budget, what happens to public saving?
What does the term “crowding out” refer to?
options: #A fall in investment due to government borrowing
A rise in saving due to lower taxes
A decline in exports due to currency appreciation
A reduction in consumption from higher income

questions.text: How can government borrowing lead to crowding out?
If a popular TV show on personal finance convinces people to save more for retirement, the ________ curve for loanable funds would shift, driving the equilibrium interest rate ________.
If the business community becomes more optimistic about the profitability of capital, the ________ curve for loanable funds would shift, driving the equilibrium interest rate ________.
Which of the following policy actions would unambiguously reduce the supply of loanable funds and crowd out investment?
options: an increase in taxes and a decrease in government spending
#a decrease in taxes together with an increase in government spending
an increase in both taxes and government spending
a decrease in both taxes and government spending

questions.text: What is present value?
options: The specific amount of funds currently held within a personal savings account
#The current worth of a future sum of money discounted for interest rates
The overall financial worth of various assets circulating within an economic system
The projected accumulation of capital after being placed into an investment over time

questions.text: What is compounding?
options: A method used to measure the expansion rate of a nation's total economic output
#The gradual increase in the value of an investment due to the reinvestment of earned interest
Obtaining financial resources from a lending institution for various expenditures
The procedure involved in making additional shares of a company available to investors

questions.text: The rule of 70 is used to:
options: A method used to analyze changes in price levels within an economy over time
#Estimate how long it takes for an investment to double in value
A technique for evaluating uncertainties and potential fluctuations in financial assets
A process for assessing the yearly expansion rate of a country's total economic production

questions.text: If an investment earns a 7% annual return, approximately how long will it take to double in value using the rule of 70?
The future value of an investment depends on:
options: The number of workers in the economy

questions.text: What is risk aversion?
options: The tendency of investors to prefer higher returns over safety
#The preference for avoiding uncertainty and potential financial losses
The decision to invest only in bonds
The act of saving all income instead of investing

questions.text: How does diversification reduce financial risk?
options: By concentrating investment in a single asset
By avoiding the stock market entirely
By investing only in government bonds

questions.text: The function of insurance in finance is primarily to:
options: Completely remove any possibility of financial loss in investments
#Reduce risk by pooling resources from multiple individuals
Offer different ways for individuals and businesses to allocate capital for potential returns
Contribute to unpredictable fluctuations in economic and financial systems

questions.text: What is the fundamental trade-off in financial decision-making?
options: The trade-off between inflation and interest rates
The trade-off between government spending and private investment
The trade-off between nominal and real GDP

questions.text: What does standard deviation measure in finance?
options: The average return of an investment
#The risk or volatility of an investment’s return
The present value of future earnings
The difference between real and nominal GDP

questions.text: If you invest $500 today and it grows by 10% per year, about how much will it be worth in 10 years? Use a simple interest formula.
If the interest rate is zero, then $100 to be paid in 10 years has a present value that is
If the interest rate is 10 percent, then the future value in 2 years of $100 today is
If the interest rate is 10 percent, then the present value of $100 to be paid in 2 years is
A student is offered this gamble: There is a 50% chance to gain $1,000 and a 50% chance to lose $1,000. The student can afford the loss, but still rejects the gamble.
options: #The possible loss creates a larger drop in satisfaction than the possible gain creates an increase in satisfaction.
The expected monetary value of the gamble is negative, so rejecting it increases the student’s average wealth over time.
The student believes inflation will reduce the future purchasing power of any winnings, so the gain is not worth the risk.
The student expects interest rates to fall, so keeping money in cash today is safer than taking any risk.

questions.text: Before an insurance contract is signed, some individuals know they are high-risk and are more likely to buy insurance than low-risk individuals. What problem does this situation illustrate?
options: People change behavior after obtaining coverage.
#High-risk individuals are more likely to purchase insurance.
Insurers increase premiums after observing losses.
Borrowers repay loans earlier than expected.

questions.text: After purchasing full car insurance, a driver becomes less careful because any damage will be covered. What problem does this situation illustrate?
options: Insurers cannot observe risk before the contract.

questions.text: Which option is an example for adverse selection?
options: Jasur begins smoking in bed after buying fire insurance.
After purchasing comprehensive health insurance, Dilshod starts visiting private clinics for minor issues and requests expensive medical tests that he previously avoided.
#Anvar is a fast driver who often breaks speed limits and has been involved in accidents before. To feel more secure, he purchases full car insurance.
When Diyor parks his BYD, he doesn’t bother putting the top up, because his insurance covers theft of any items left in the car.

questions.text: The ability of insurance to spread risk is limited by
The benefit of diversification when constructing a portfolio is that it can eliminate
The extra return that stocks earn over bonds (on average) compensates stockholders for
options: # the greater market risk that stockholding entails.
the greater firm-specific risk that stockholding entails.
the higher taxes levied on stockholders.
the higher brokerage costs incurred buying stocks.

questions.text: A company earns a profit at the end of the year and decides to distribute part of that profit to its shareholders instead of keeping all of it for future investment. What is this payment called?
options: #A dividend paid from company profits to shareholders.
An interest payment made regularly to bondholders.
A capital gain earned from selling company shares.
A loan provided by investors to the company.

questions.text: According to the efficient markets hypothesis, what causes stock prices to change?
If stock prices follow a random walk, what does this imply for investors using public information?
options: They can predict prices with charts.
They can consistently earn excess returns.
They can forecast profits using trends.

questions.text: Which statement challenges the assumption of fully rational markets?
A market is said to experience a speculative bubble when:
How is the unemployment rate calculated?
The labor force consists of:
options: All people in the country, including children
Only those who have a job
#The total number of employed and unemployed workers
Only people who are actively looking for jobs

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT one of the three main types of unemployment?
If a person is unemployed because they are in between jobs, this is known as:
Which type of unemployment is most closely associated with economic downturns?
The natural rate of unemployment consists of:
Which of the following government policies is most likely to reduce frictional unemployment?
options: Adjusting baseline earnings to ensure workers receive higher compensation for their labor
#Enhancing support systems that help individuals find suitable employment opportunities more efficiently
Implementing monetary policies that lead to higher borrowing costs within the economy
Cutting back on public sector expenditures to influence overall economic activity

questions.text: Structural unemployment occurs when:
options: #Workers lack the necessary skills for available jobs
There are not enough jobs during a recession
Wages are flexible and adjust quickly

questions.text: The labor force participation rate is calculated as:
If discouraged workers re-enter the labor force to look for jobs, what happens to the unemployment rate?
Which of the following would be classified as a discouraged worker?
options: Someone who lost their job and is actively applying for work
A college graduate searching for their first job
#Someone who has stopped looking for work because they believe no jobs are available
A worker who has taken a part-time job but wants full-time work

questions.text: If the labor force is 160 million and 8 million people are unemployed, what is the unemployment rate?
Suppose the adult population is 250 million, the labor force is 150 million, and the number of unemployed is 10 million. What is the labor force participation rate?
If inflation rises unexpectedly, how does it affect the real wages of workers with fixed wages?

A country has the following data:

Labor force: 140 million

Employed: 130 million

What is the unemployment rate?
Why can a labor union be described as a cartel?
options: #It coordinates workers to gain market power.
It reduces wages to increase employment.
It competes with firms for customers.
It sets prices for final goods.

questions.text: What is collective bargaining?
Why might a firm agree to higher wages during a strike threat?
According to the theory of efficiency wages,
options: #firms may find it profitable to pay above equilibrium wages.
an excess supply of labor puts downward pressure on wages.
sectoral shifts are the main source of frictional unemployment.
right-to-work laws reduce the bargaining power of unions.

questions.text: When a firm pays an efficiency wage, it may
options: have to monitor its workers more closely.
#find that its workers quit less frequently.

questions.text: What is money?
options: Any physical material or resource that holds measurable economic worth
#Any item that is widely accepted as payment for goods and services
Only precious metals, such as gold and silver, traditionally used as a store of value
The overall financial assets and resources available within a nation's economic system

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a function of money?
What is included in M1 money supply?
options: Large time deposits and corporate bonds

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of fiat money?
Which institution is primarily responsible for controlling the money supply in Uzbekistan?
options: The Ministry of Economy and Finance

questions.text: The Central bank controls the money supply primarily through:
What happens when the central bank buys government bonds from the public?
Which of the following is an example of commodity money?
If the required reserve ratio is 5%, what is the maximum potential money supply expansion from a $1,200 deposit?
